book_claims.claim: Relatively high concentration of vegetable omega-3 fatty acids
book_claims.mechanism: 50g fresh purslane contains approximately 200mg alpha-linolenic acid

supplementary_research.finding: Purslane is the richest source of alpha-linolenic acid (ALA) of any leafy vegetable examined, containing 300-400 mg ALA per 100g fresh weight. Also contains significant EPA (omega-3), unusual for a land plant.

Comprehensive review of purslane: contains melatonin (highest of any vegetable tested), beta-carotene, vitamin C, and glutathione. Demonstrates anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective, and anti-diabetic properties in animal models.
Purslane extracts showed potent antioxidant activity and reduced inflammatory markers (TNF-alpha, IL-6) in cell models. The omega-3 and polyphenol content work synergistically.

culinary_pairings.source: Classic Turkish salad; purslane and tomato with olive oil is a summer staple
Fat enhances absorption of fat-soluble omega-3 ALA and carotenoids
Fresh salad combination; both are cooling and hydrating summer vegetables
Purslane with yogurt (yoğurtlu semizotu) is a classic Turkish meze
Acid complements purslane's own tartness; enhances iron absorption
Adds depth to purslane salads; complementary organosulfur compounds
Both rich in omega-3 ALA; combined in Georgian pkhali preparations

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Richest leafy vegetable source of ALA. Conversion to EPA/DHA is low (~5%) but purslane uniquely also contains some preformed EPA. Fat co-ingestion improves absorption.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: Up to 12 mcg (highest of any vegetable)
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Dietary melatonin is bioavailable and may contribute to antioxidant defense and circadian rhythm support.
Fat-soluble. Higher than many cultivated salad greens.
Master intracellular antioxidant. Oral bioavailability is debated; may support gut antioxidant status directly.
Good bioavailability. Complements glutathione's antioxidant function by recycling oxidized glutathione.
